title: GENTLEMEN OF FORTUNE (ДЖЕНТЛЬМЕНЫ УДАЧИ, 1971) — POLL QUOTES ABOVE 2%

original.rus: Я злой и страшный серый волк, я в поросятах знаю толк!

general.text:
The joke is that the line is delivered not by a real villain or criminal, but by the kind, soft kindergarten director. He is performing “scariness” for children, and the comedy lies in the safe, theatrical artificiality of it: the words claim predatory menace, but the setting makes it harmless and playful.

The phrase compresses several recognizable things: the fairy-tale “big bad wolf,” the Three Little Pigs frame, a mock-boastful rhyme, and Leonov/Troshkin’s deliberately exaggerated performance. Its meme value is not just “wolf = scary.” It is “I am pretending to be scary, but everyone understands this is a child-safe performance.”

As a meme, it is used for comic self-presentation as dangerous, predatory, experienced, or “bad,” especially in obviously unserious contexts. It can also be used when someone wants to overact villainy in a cartoonish, childish way. The rhyme «волк / толк» helps: it is compact, chant-like, and easy to detach from the film while still sounding performative.

The phrase is funny because it is an extremely brutal threat, but phrased in such stylized, almost cartoonish criminal slang that it becomes performative rather than realistic. «Пасть» instead of neutral “рот” and «моргалы» instead of “глаза” push the line into a rough, archaic / underworld register. It sounds violent, but also grotesquely theatrical.

In the film, this is part of the first construction of Docent as a terrifying criminal authority. The line works because it is compact, rhythmic, and excessive: two body-part threats in a row, both vivid and absurdly physical. Later, when variants of this formula are repeated by or around Troshkin, the phrase becomes detached from real danger and turns into a comic password for “criminal toughness.”

As a meme, it is often used as mock-aggression: when someone wants to jokingly threaten, scold, or dramatize irritation without intending real violence.

original.rus: Рога поотшибаю, пасть порву, моргалы выколю! Всю жизнь работать на лекарства будешь...

general.text:
This is the expanded, maximal version of the Docent threat. Compared with the shorter «Пасть порву, моргалы выколю», it is funnier because it keeps accumulating violence past the point of credibility: first “рога,” then “пасть,” then “моргалы,” then the bureaucratically practical consequence — the victim will spend his whole life earning money for medicine.

The phrase works as a meme because it is not just a threat; it is a whole comic performance of criminal authority. The language is rough and bodily, but also oddly inventive. «Рога», «пасть», «моргалы» make the victim sound less like a human and more like an animal or cartoon creature. Then «всю жизнь работать на лекарства будешь» shifts from immediate violence to absurd long-term financial planning, which makes the threat grotesquely funny.

In everyday use, the phrase tends to mark exaggerated mock-rage: a comic way to say “I’ll destroy you” when the situation is not actually serious. It is especially usable when someone wants to sound like a cartoon gangster, not a real aggressor. The humor depends on excess: the speaker is not simply angry, but hilariously over-equipped with threats.

original.rus: Сколько я зарезал, сколько перерезал, сколько душ я загубил!

general.text:
The line is funny because it is not spoken as an ordinary threat or confession: Docent sings it. That immediately pushes the content into grotesque performance. The words are murderous and melodramatic, but the delivery makes them sound like a dark little folk / prison-song fragment, almost a self-mythologizing refrain.

The humor depends on the clash between content and form. The content is violent: «зарезал», «перерезал», «душ загубил». But the form is rhythmic, singable, and exaggerated. He is not merely saying “I am dangerous”; he is theatrically enjoying the role of a legendary criminal. The rhyme-like sequence makes the line memorable and detachable from the scene.

In the film, this helps build Docent as a figure of criminal folklore rather than realistic violence. He is frightening inside the plot, but comically stylized for the viewer. The phrase belongs to the same system as «Пасть порву»: threat as performance.

As a meme, it is used for exaggerated self-incrimination or mock villainy: “look how many terrible things I’ve supposedly done.” Because it is sung, it can also be used when someone wants to dramatize guilt in a deliberately theatrical, unserious way. The meme effect is less “real confession” than “comic aria of villainy.”

original.rus: Этот Василий Алибабаевич... этот нехороший человек... мне на ногу батарею сбросил, падла!

general.text:
The phrase is funny because it starts in artificially polite, “translated” language and then collapses back into raw insult. Fedya begins with the corrected civilian formulation — «нехороший человек» — which echoes Troshkin’s effort to replace criminal slang with acceptable speech. But he cannot sustain the polite register, so the line ends with «падла». The joke is in that failure of re-education.

It also works because of the specificity of the grievance. «Мне на ногу батарею сбросил» is not a grand criminal betrayal, but a very physical, stupid, painful domestic injury. The combination of formal full name — «Василий Алибабаевич» — moral euphemism — «нехороший человек» — and crude final curse creates a perfect register crash.

As a meme, the phrase is useful when someone wants to complain about another person while pretending to be restrained or polite, but then slipping into anger anyway. The structure is highly reusable: “this X, this bad person, did Y to me, bastard.” It is especially funny for petty but personally painful offenses, where the speaker’s indignation is disproportionate but understandable.

original.rus: Украл, выпил - в тюрьму! Украл, выпил - в тюрьму! Романтика!

general.text:
The line is funny because Troshkin, a kindergarten director, is trying to speak as if he understands the criminal worldview from inside. But what he produces is not a glamorous outlaw philosophy; it is a brutally repetitive life cycle reduced to a simple formula: steal, drink, prison; steal, drink, prison. The punchline «Романтика!» is comic because it labels this miserable mechanical loop as if it were an attractive adventure.

In the film, the phrase belongs to Troshkin’s moral-pedagogical strategy. He is impersonating Docent, but he is also exposing the emptiness of the criminal life. The rhythm is important: the repeated sequence sounds almost like a slogan or lesson. It is both a parody of criminal “romance” and a diagnosis.

As a meme, it is used when describing a repetitive, self-defeating cycle while pretending to admire it. The structure is very portable: “did X, got consequence Y; did X, got consequence Y; romance!” It usually signals bitter irony: a supposedly exciting lifestyle turns out to be predictable, stupid, and bleak.

original.rus: Все! Кина не будет. Электричество кончилось.

general.text:
The line is funny because it sounds like a rough, mock-official announcement cancelling a screening or performance. «Кина» instead of «кино» gives it a deliberately nonstandard, rustic / low-register flavor, as if Fedya is parodying a provincial projectionist or club worker.

The second half is just as important: «электричество кончилось» is not the neutral way to say the electricity went out. One expects something like «электричество отключили», «свет погас», «света нет». With «кончилось», electricity is treated like a consumable substance: sugar, bread, money, patience. That odd verb choice makes the excuse sound both childish and absurdly practical.

In the film, Fedya uses the phrase to terminate the kindergarten-style performance. He does not play along with the theatrical / children’s-world register; he shuts it down with a crude pseudo-technical explanation. This matches his character: impatient, literal-minded, anti-sentimental, but comic in how clumsily he speaks.

As a meme, it means “the show is over,” “nothing will happen,” or “the expected spectacle is cancelled.” It is often used to deflate anticipation or drama with a blunt, absurdly mundane reason. The phrase works especially well when something ends not with a grand failure, but with a flat, ridiculous “technical” excuse.

original.rus: Лошадью ходи, век воли не видать!

general.text:
The joke starts from chess terminology. In proper Russian chess language the piece is «конь», and the move would be «ходи конём». Fedya says «лошадью ходи», which is understandable but lower-register and comically unschooled. It makes the chess scene sound less like intellectual play and more like rough street/prison improvisation.

The second half, «век воли не видать», imports criminal-oath language into a harmless chess game. Instead of giving calm advice, Fedya swears on prison freedom that the knight move is correct. That mismatch is the core joke: a board-game move is treated with the emotional seriousness of underworld fate.

In the scene, the line also shows how the criminals drag every social situation into their own register. Chess, normally associated with thought and culture, becomes noisy, coercive, and quasi-criminal. The advice may even be tactically right, but it is delivered in the wrong social language.

As a meme, the phrase is used mostly when someone is giving forceful, overconfident advice, especially about a “clever move” or unexpected maneuver. It can also be used more generally to mean: “make the unconventional move; trust me, this is the move.”

original.rus: Кто же его посадит? Он же памятник!

general.text:
The joke is a literal misunderstanding. The others are trying to identify a monument and ask whether the figure is sitting — «сидит?» Fedya hears or treats «посадить» in the everyday / legal sense: to put someone in prison. His answer collapses two meanings of the same root: “to seat” and “to imprison.” Since a monument cannot be jailed, he concludes: «Он же памятник!»

This is very characteristic of Kosoy: he is not just “stupid,” but linguistically literal-minded in a way that turns ordinary clarification into absurdity. The line also fits the film’s larger comic system, where criminal language keeps invading neutral contexts. Even a discussion of a statue is pulled toward prison logic.

As a meme, it is used in two main ways. First, literally, for jokes about monuments, especially sitting/standing statues. Second, more broadly, it means: “this person/object is untouchable; nobody will punish or remove him/it because it has symbolic status.”

The meme value is not only the pun. It also gives a compact formula for protected absurdity: someone may deserve to be “sat down,” removed, punished, or corrected, but cannot be, because they have become a “monument.”

original.rus: Это тебе не мелочь по карманам тырить...

general.text:
The phrase is funny because it frames theft as a matter of professional hierarchy. «Мелочь по карманам тырить» means petty pickpocketing or stealing coins from pockets; the speaker contrasts that with the more serious criminal enterprise around the golden helmet. In other words: this is not amateur pocket change, this is “real work.”

The comic effect comes from the mock-professional tone. Khmyr sounds as if he is giving practical career advice inside the criminal world: do not confuse small-time theft with a major operation. The lowered verb «тырить» is crucial; it is casual, street-level, and contemptuous, so the phrase immediately establishes a criminal but non-grandiose register.

As a meme, it is used when someone wants to emphasize that a task is not trivial or amateur-level: “this is not just some small thing.” It can be applied jokingly to any situation where the speaker wants to inflate the seriousness of the undertaking, especially if the contrast is between petty messing around and a more complex job. The humor is in treating ordinary difficulty as if it were a criminal qualification exam.

The joke is built on a false display of competence. Troshkin has assigned the men English, and Khmyr produces something that starts like English — «Йес, йес» — but then collapses into a very Soviet abbreviation: ОБХСС, the department for combating theft of socialist property and speculation in the Soviet police system.

The phrase is funny because it short-circuits two incompatible registers: beginner textbook English and Soviet law-enforcement bureaucracy. It sounds as if Khmyr has learned the rhythm of a foreign language but fills it with the only institutional vocabulary that matters in his world: police, theft, economic crime.

As a meme, it works when someone pretends to know English, corporate/international jargon, or some prestigious code, but immediately reveals a very local or bureaucratic mindset. It can also be used more generally for absurd code-switching: a bit of English-gloss followed by a heavy Soviet acronym. The line is small, but it captures one of the film’s main comic mechanisms: re-education as a language lesson that the criminals keep dragging back into their own world.

original.rus: Эй, гражданина! Ты туда не ходи, ты сюда ходи! А то снег башка попадет, совсем мертвый будешь!

general.text:
The phrase works as a comic public-safety warning. The danger itself is real: snow or ice falling from roofs can seriously injure or kill someone. The humor is in the form of the warning, not in the absence of danger.

Vasily’s speech is practical, caring, and grammatically marked at the same time: «гражданина» as an address, «снег башка попадет», simplified syntax, and the blunt result «совсем мертвый будешь». He is not threatening the passerby; he is urgently redirecting them away from danger. The line therefore combines command, concern, broken Russian, and fatalistic directness.

As a meme, it is used for warning someone away from a dangerous, stupid, or undesirable path: “don’t go there, go here, otherwise things will end badly.” It is especially natural in winter contexts with snow, icicles, roofs, or unsafe sidewalks, but it also works metaphorically for any situation where the speaker gives rough but well-meant advice.

original.rus: А в тюрьме сейчас ужин... макароны!

general.text:
The phrase is funny because it reverses the expected logic of escape. The characters have just escaped from prison, so freedom should be better than anything left behind. But Vasily suddenly thinks about the prison dinner and regrets missing it. The word «макароны» makes the moment especially banal: this is not nostalgia for safety, friendship, or home, but for a simple institutional meal.

The comedy also depends on Vasily’s innocence and bodily practicality. He is not making a philosophical point about prison life; he is hungry. His mind goes directly to the scheduled routine of prison food. That makes the line both absurd and human.

As a meme, it is used when someone has supposedly left a bad situation but remembers one small comfort from it. It can also be used more generally for comic misplaced priorities: in the middle of danger, stress, or freedom, the person thinks about food. The phrase works because «тюрьма» and «ужин» belong to incompatible emotional frames, and «макароны» makes the contrast even flatter and funnier.

original.rus: — А ты зачем бежал? — Все побежали и я побежал.

general.text:
The phrase is funny because it reduces a serious act — escape from prison — to herd behavior. The question asks for motive, but Vasily gives no motive at all: he ran because others ran. That makes the jailbreak sound less like a planned criminal decision and more like a child joining a crowd.

The line fits Vasily’s character: he is not driven by criminal strategy so much as by simple, immediate logic. His answer is absurdly honest. He does not invent a justification, ideology, or excuse; he states the social mechanism plainly.

As a meme, it is used for situations where someone joins an action without understanding why: following a trend, panic, queue, protest, workplace initiative, group decision, or collective mistake. It usually carries gentle self-irony: “I didn’t think it through; everyone else did it, so I did too.”

The phrase is funny because it combines three incompatible registers in one short announcement. «Кушать подано» is ceremonially polite, almost servant-like or restaurant-like. «Садитесь жрать» is crude and animalizing. «Пожалуйста» then restores politeness at the very end, but too late: the collision has already happened.

This is a classic register-crash joke. Vasily is trying to perform hospitality properly, but his language cannot keep a stable social level. The result is not simple rudeness: it is rude content inside a polite frame. That makes the phrase warmer and funnier than a direct insult.

As a meme, it is used when inviting people to eat in a deliberately mock-formal / mock-crude way. More generally, it works for any situation where someone offers something politely but cannot resist using blunt, low-register wording. Its meme value is in the rhythm: ceremonial opening, vulgar core, polite closing.

This is the film’s most compact threat-formula. Its force comes from being short, bodily, and rough: «пасть» is not neutral “mouth,” but an animalizing, low-register word, so the threat sounds primitive and physical rather than legally or psychologically sophisticated.

In the film, the phrase first belongs to the real Docent and establishes criminal menace. Because it is so brief and repeatable, it later becomes a role-marker: to sound like Docent, say «пасть порву». The threat turns into a linguistic costume.

As a meme, it is used as a generic comic threat: exaggerated anger, mock intimidation, or theatrical “don’t mess with me.” Compared with the longer «Пасть порву, моргалы выколю», this shorter version is especially reusable because it is almost all gesture: a blunt warning, a comic growl, or a shorthand for “I am switching into Docent mode.”

original.rus: — Девушка, а как Вас зовут? — Таня. — А меня Федя. — Ну и дура.

general.text:
The joke depends on the disguise. Fedya is dressed as a woman, so Tanya initially interprets him as a woman asking her name. The exchange starts like a normal polite encounter: «Девушка, а как Вас зовут?» / «Таня.» But then Fedya says «А меня Федя», which makes the situation suddenly absurd: from Tanya’s point of view, a “woman” has just introduced herself with an obviously male name.

That is why «Ну и дура» lands as the punchline. Tanya is not responding to a normal flirtation; she is reacting to what appears to her as a bizarre and stupid self-presentation. She does not analyze the disguise or ask questions. She simply concludes that this “woman” is an idiot.

As a meme, the exchange is used for failed self-presentation, especially when someone’s attempt to introduce themselves or make contact contains an obvious mismatch. Its comic logic is: the speaker offers information meant to establish identity, but the information instead makes them look ridiculous.

original.rus: Деточка, а вам не кажется, что ваше место возле параши?

general.text:
The joke comes from the collision between mock-politeness and prison insult. «Деточка» and «вам не кажется» sound almost courteous, even patronizingly refined. But the content is a brutal prison-world humiliation: «ваше место возле параши». The phrase is structured like a polite social correction, while actually assigning the addressee the lowest possible status.

In the scene, this also tests Troshkin’s authority. Nikola Pitersky is speaking as an experienced criminal to someone he thinks is Docent or is trying to challenge as Docent. The insult is not just vulgar; it is hierarchical. It says: you do not belong among those with power; your place is by the toilet bucket.

As a meme, it is used for contemptuous dismissal while pretending to remain civil. The speaker can use it when someone is behaving above their station, taking the wrong place, or trying to enter a conversation where they are not wanted. The meme force lies in the elegant opening and degrading ending: the line begins like etiquette and ends like prison.

The joke comes from the sudden reversal of status. Nikola Pitersky has just spoken in a prison-insult register, trying to assert dominance. But when threatened with «моргалы выколю», he immediately switches into the voice of a helpless victim calling for public protection: «Помогите!»

The phrase «зрения лишают» is funny because it is both literal and absurdly official-sounding. Instead of simply shouting “they’re poking my eyes out,” he formulates it almost like a bureaucratic injury report: someone is depriving him of eyesight. That formal phrasing makes the panic comic.

As a meme, it is used when someone theatrically complains that they are being harmed, overwhelmed, or visually assaulted — especially by something unpleasant to look at. It can also be used more generally as mock-victim language: the speaker is not necessarily in serious danger, but dramatizes the situation as if “hooligans” were committing an outrage against them.
